section.title: At the Feast of Tabernacles
section.sectionRationale: At the autumn Feast of Tabernacles the tension around Jesus sharpens into open division. His own brothers do not believe; the crowds whisper; the leaders send officers to arrest Him. In the middle of it all Jesus teaches that His message is from the Father, and on the great final day, amid the feast's water ceremony, He cries out the offer of living water, the Spirit for all who believe. The crowd and the council are split, and even the arresting officers come back empty-handed and amazed.
section.thesis: At the Feast of Tabernacles Jesus teaches with an authority that is from the Father and not His own, and on the last great day He offers the living water of the Spirit to all who thirst and believe, so that the feast becomes the setting for a decisive division over whether He is the Christ.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the unbelief of Jesus' brothers and His going up in secret; His temple teaching on whose glory He seeks and why they seek to kill Him; the debate over whether He is the Christ and His words about going away; and the offer of living water, the division in the crowd, and the leaders' contempt answered by Nicodemus.

units.label: My time has not yet come
units.note: even His brothers did not believe

verses.text: After this, Jesus traveled throughout Galilee. He did not want to travel in Judea, because the Jews there were trying to kill Him.
However, the Jewish Feast of Tabernacles was near.
So Jesus’ brothers said to Him, “Leave here and go to Judea, so that Your disciples there may see the works You are doing.
For no one who wants to be known publicly acts in secret. Since You are doing these things, show Yourself to the world.”
For even His own brothers did not believe in Him.
Therefore Jesus told them, “Although your time is always at hand, My time has not yet come.
The world cannot hate you, but it hates Me, because I testify that its works are evil.
Go up to the feast on your own. I am not going up to this feast, because My time has not yet come.”
Having said this, Jesus remained in Galilee.
But after His brothers had gone up to the feast, He also went, not publicly, but in secret.

units.label: Teaching in the temple
units.note: My teaching is not My own

verses.text: So the Jews were looking for Him at the feast and asking, “Where is He?”
Many in the crowds were whispering about Him. Some said, “He is a good man.” But others replied, “No, He deceives the people.”
Yet no one would speak publicly about Him for fear of the Jews.
About halfway through the feast, Jesus went up to the temple courts and began to teach.
The Jews were amazed and asked, “How did this man attain such learning without having studied?”
“My teaching is not My own,” Jesus replied. “It comes from Him who sent Me.
If anyone desires to do His will, he will know whether My teaching is from God or whether I speak on My own.
He who speaks on his own authority seeks his own glory, but He who seeks the {{doxa|glory}} of the One who sent Him is a man of truth; in Him there is no falsehood.
Has not Moses given you the law? Yet not one of you keeps it. Why are you trying to kill Me?”
“You have a demon,” the crowd replied. “Who is trying to kill You?”
Jesus answered them, “I did one miracle, and you are all amazed.
But because Moses gave you circumcision, you circumcise a boy on the Sabbath (not that it is from Moses, but from the patriarchs.)
If a boy can be circumcised on the Sabbath so that the law of Moses will not be broken, why are you angry with Me for making the whole man well on the Sabbath?
Stop judging by outward appearances, and start judging justly.”

units.label: Is Jesus the Christ?
units.note: where I am, you cannot come

verses.text: Then some of the people of Jerusalem began to say, “Isn’t this the man they are trying to kill?
Yet here He is, speaking publicly, and they are not saying anything to Him. Have the rulers truly recognized that this is the Christ?
But we know where this man is from. When the Christ comes, no one will know where He is from.”
Then Jesus, still teaching in the temple courts, cried out, “You know Me, and you know where I am from. I have not come of My own accord, but He who sent Me is true. You do not know Him,
but I know Him, because I am from Him and He sent Me.”
So they tried to seize Him, but no one laid a hand on Him, because His {{hora|hour}} had not yet come.
Many in the crowd, however, believed in Him and said, “When the Christ comes, will He perform more signs than this man?”
When the Pharisees heard the crowd whispering these things about Jesus, they and the chief priests sent officers to arrest Him.
So Jesus said, “I am with you only a little while longer, and then I am going to the One who sent Me.
You will look for Me, but you will not find Me; and where I am, you cannot come.”
At this, the Jews said to one another, “Where does He intend to go that we will not find Him? Will He go where the Jews are dispersed among the Greeks, and teach the Greeks?
What does He mean by saying, ‘You will look for Me, but you will not find Me,’ and, ‘Where I am, you cannot come’?”

units.label: Rivers of living water
units.note: the offer of the Spirit and the division it brings

verses.text: On the last and greatest day of the feast, Jesus stood up and called out in a loud voice, “If anyone is thirsty, let him come to Me and drink.
Whoever believes in Me, as the Scripture has said: ‘Streams of {{hydorzon|living water}} will flow from within him.’”
He was speaking about the {{pneuma|Spirit}}, whom those who believed in Him were later to receive. For the Spirit had not yet been given, because Jesus had not yet been glorified.
On hearing these words, some of the people said, “This is truly the Prophet.”
Others declared, “This is the Christ.” But still others asked, “How can the Christ come from Galilee?
Doesn’t the Scripture say that the Christ will come from the line of David and from Bethlehem, the village where David lived?”
So there was division in the crowd because of Jesus.
Some of them wanted to seize Him, but no one laid a hand on Him.
Then the officers returned to the chief priests and Pharisees, who asked them, “Why didn’t you bring Him in?”
“Never has anyone spoken like this man!” the officers answered.
“Have you also been deceived?” replied the Pharisees.
“Have any of the rulers or Pharisees believed in Him?
But this crowd that does not know the law, they are under a curse.”
Nicodemus, who had gone to Jesus earlier and who himself was one of them, asked,
“Does our law convict a man without first hearing from him to determine what he has done?”
“Aren’t you also from Galilee?” they replied. “Look into it, and you will see that no prophet comes out of Galilee.”

doxa.gloss: Glory, honor, reputation. Jesus draws a line between the false teacher who seeks his own glory and the true one who seeks the glory of the One who sent him. Because Jesus seeks the Father's glory rather than His own, His words carry the ring of truth. In John, glory finally shines at the cross, the hour when the Son is glorified.

hora.gloss: Hour, appointed time. Twice in this chapter the crowd cannot seize Jesus because His hour has not yet come. In John the hour is the fixed, Father-appointed moment of His death and glorification; until it arrives no human hand can touch Him, and when it comes He lays down His life willingly. His timing, not His enemies', governs the story.

hydorzon.gloss: Living water. At the feast's water-drawing ceremony Jesus cries that whoever believes in Him will have streams of living water flowing from within. It is the same gift He offered the Samaritan woman, now identified plainly as the Spirit. The believer not only drinks but becomes a channel of life to others.

pneuma.gloss: Spirit. John explains that the living water is the Holy Spirit, whom believers would receive once Jesus was glorified. The gift of the Spirit is tied to the completion of Jesus' work in death, resurrection, and exaltation; only then are the rivers of living water poured out on all who believe.

basic.context: The setting is the Feast of Tabernacles, an autumn pilgrim feast recalling Israel's wilderness journey and celebrated with daily ceremonies of water and light. Jesus' own brothers, not yet believing, urge Him to go public in Judea. He goes up quietly, then teaches openly in the temple, insisting His message comes from the Father, not Himself. The crowd is split, some think He is the Christ, others object that He comes from Galilee, and the leaders send guards to arrest Him. On the climactic day Jesus stands and offers living water, the Spirit, to all who thirst. The guards return unable to arrest a man who speaks like no one else.
basic.hermeneutics: Notice the theme of division: the same Jesus draws faith from some and hatred from others, and the feast becomes a courtroom where people judge Him and are judged by their response. The key questions are, is His teaching from God, and where does the Christ come from. Jesus answers that anyone willing to do God's will can recognize His teaching, and He offers Himself as the source of the Spirit. The living water is explicitly the Spirit, given after Jesus is glorified.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on several terms. Jesus seeks not His own doxa, glory, but the Father's, which marks Him as true. His enemies cannot seize Him because His hora, His hour, has not yet come. On the last day He offers hydor zon, living water, which John identifies as the pneuma, the Spirit, to be given once Jesus is glorified.
basic.typology: The Feast of Tabernacles, with its water-drawing ceremony recalling water from the rock, is fulfilled as Jesus offers the living water of the Spirit. The wilderness feast that looked back to God's provision now points to the greater provision in Christ.
basic.christ: Christ is the sent one whose teaching is the Father's, who seeks the Father's glory, and who cannot be seized until His hour. He is the source of living water, the Spirit, for all who thirst and believe. The whole feast finds its meaning in Him.
basic.god: God is the Father who sends the Son and whose glory the Son seeks. He is true, and He governs the timing of events so that no one can touch the Son until the appointed hour. He is the giver of the Spirit through the glorified Christ.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 17 and Numbers 20 recall water from the rock. Zechariah 14 links Tabernacles with living waters flowing from Jerusalem. Micah 5 foretells the Christ from Bethlehem. John 4 first offered the living water.
basic.oneStory: At the feast that remembered God's provision in the wilderness, Jesus reveals Himself as the true rock from which living water flows, the Spirit for all who believe. The division He provokes runs through the whole story, as some receive the sent Son and others reject Him, unable even to see where the Christ truly comes from.

context.literary: A chapter built on movement and division: Jesus' hidden going up, His temple teaching, the crowd's debate, the failed arrest, and the climactic offer of living water. Whispered opinions, official plots, and popular confusion are interwoven, and the narrator supplies the key interpretive note in verse 39 that the living water is the Spirit. The section ends with the council's contempt and Nicodemus's cautious defense, setting up the next scenes.
context.historical: Tabernacles, or Sukkot, was one of the three great pilgrim feasts, recalling the wilderness wanderings when Israel lived in booths. Its daily ritual included a priestly procession drawing water from the pool of Siloam and pouring it out at the altar, praying for rain and recalling water from the rock, and great lamps lit the temple court. Jesus' cries about living water and, in the next chapter, light, deliberately draw on these ceremonies. Rabbinic expectation about the Messiah's hidden origin lies behind the crowd's debate.
context.canonical: The living water fulfills the Tabernacles imagery of Zechariah 14:8, living waters flowing from Jerusalem, and the rock in the wilderness, which 1 Corinthians 10:4 identifies with Christ. The explicit link of the water to the Spirit given after Jesus' glorification anticipates Pentecost, Acts 2, and ties the gift of the Spirit to the completion of Christ's saving work. The debate over Bethlehem echoes Micah 5:2.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show Jesus teaching with authority from the Father amid mounting hostility, to present His offer of the Spirit as living water for all who thirst and believe, and to display the division His coming forces, so that readers will judge rightly and come to Him.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes the events of one feast and the reactions they provoked. The abiding teaching is prescriptive: Jesus' word is from God and recognized by those willing to do God's will, and He is the source of the Spirit for all who thirst, come, and believe.

debates.question: Why did Jesus say He was not going up to the feast and then go?

views.label: Not going up publicly or on their terms
views.case: Jesus declines to go up in the public, self-promoting way His brothers urged; He goes later, quietly, so He is not going up in the sense they meant, on their timetable and manner.

views.label: A matter of the appointed time
views.case: His refrain My time has not yet come governs the scene; He will not act on human prompting but only as the Father directs, so He goes when and how the Father wills, not when pressed.

debates.bearing: Both readings clear Jesus of duplicity: He refused to go up in the manner and timing demanded, and acted instead on the Father's schedule, which is the whole point of the hour in John.

debates.question: From whom do the rivers of living water flow, the believer or Christ?

views.label: From within the believer
views.case: The natural reading of verse 38 is that streams flow from within the one who believes, so that the Spirit-filled believer becomes a source of life to others, drinking and then overflowing.

views.label: From Christ Himself
views.case: Some punctuate so that the living water flows from Christ, the true rock and temple, to whom the thirsty come and drink; the Scripture imagery of water from the sanctuary supports this.

debates.bearing: Either way the source of the water is Christ and the water is the Spirit; whether stressing Christ as fountain or the believer as channel, the gift flows from Jesus to and through those who believe.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter is shaped by several terms. Jesus insists His teaching is not His own but the Father's, and He seeks not His own doxa, glory, but the glory of the One who sent Him, which marks Him as a man of truth. Twice His enemies fail to seize Him because His hora, His hour, has not yet come; the Father's appointed time, not human plotting, governs events. On the last and greatest day of the feast, at the water ceremony, Jesus cries out the offer of hydor zon, living water, and John explains plainly that He meant the pneuma, the Spirit, whom believers would receive once Jesus was glorified.

typology.type: Water from the rock in the wilderness, Exodus 17 and Numbers 20
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true rock, from whom the living water of the Spirit flows to all who thirst, 1 Corinthians 10:4 and John 7:37-39.

typology.type: The Tabernacles water ceremony and Zechariah 14:8
typology.fulfillment: The living waters flowing out to the world through the glorified Christ and His Spirit-filled people.
God's provision for His pilgrim people fulfilled in Christ, who gives the Spirit to sustain them to the end.

study.christ: Christ appears as the sent Son whose teaching is the Father's own, who seeks the Father's glory and not His own, and who therefore speaks with unmatched truth, so that even the arresting officers confess no one ever spoke like Him. He is untouchable until His hour, master of the story's timing. Above all He is the giver of living water: standing at the height of the feast He offers Himself as the source of the Spirit for all who thirst, come, and believe, the true rock from which the rivers of life flow to a dry world.
study.god: The Father is the sender whose glory the Son seeks and whose will is the test by which His teaching is known. He is true, and He governs the times, so that no hand can seize the Son until the appointed hour. He is the giver of the Spirit, poured out through the glorified Christ upon all who believe, fulfilling the feast's ancient prayers for water and life. The division at the feast reveals hearts, but the Father is drawing a people to His Son and to the living water only He can give.

crossReferences.note: Living waters will flow out from Jerusalem, an image of Tabernacles that Jesus fulfills in offering the Spirit.
They drank from the spiritual rock that followed them, and that rock was Christ, the source of living water.
The ruler of Israel will come from Bethlehem, behind the crowd's dispute over the Christ's origin.
The Spirit poured out at Pentecost after Jesus was glorified, fulfilling the promise of living water.

study.oneStory: At the feast that remembered God's provision of water in the wilderness, Jesus stands and reveals Himself as the true rock from which living water flows, the Spirit for everyone who thirsts and believes. The gift waits on His glorification, tying the Spirit to the cross and the throne. And the division He provokes at the feast runs through the whole story of Scripture: the sent Son is received by some and rejected by others who cannot even see where the Christ truly comes from, while those who come to Him drink of the Spirit and become rivers of life to the world.

quiz.q: How does Jesus answer the question of whether His teaching is His own?

options.text: His teaching comes from the Father who sent Him, and those willing to do God's will can recognize it.
options.note: Verses 16 and 17 ground His teaching in the Father and tie recognition to a willing heart.

options.text: He admits it is His own clever invention.
options.note: The opposite; He insists the teaching is not His own but the Father's.

options.text: He says only trained scholars can judge His teaching.
options.note: He says the opposite: anyone willing to do God's will can recognize it, learned or not.

options.text: He says it comes from God and He seeks the Father's glory, not His own.
options.note: True, but it omits the key test, that those willing to do God's will will know whether it is from God.

quiz.q: Why could no one arrest Jesus at the feast, despite the attempts?

options.text: Because His hour had not yet come.
options.note: Verse 30 gives this reason; the Father's appointed time governs events.

options.text: Because the crowd protected Him.
options.note: The crowd was divided; the stated reason is that His hour had not come.

options.text: Because the officers were afraid of the people.
options.note: The officers were amazed by His words, but the text grounds it in His hour, not fear of the crowd.

options.text: Because the appointed time of His death had not arrived.
options.note: True, this is the meaning of His hour, but the answer restates it without naming the hour the text emphasizes.

quiz.q: What is the living water Jesus offers on the last day of the feast?

options.text: The Holy Spirit, given to believers after Jesus is glorified.
options.note: Verse 39 explicitly identifies the living water as the Spirit.

options.text: Literal water for the thirsty pilgrims.
options.note: The offer is spiritual; John names it as the Spirit, not literal water.

options.text: The refreshment of His teaching and truth.
options.note: His words give life, but the passage specifically names the gift as the Spirit.

options.text: A river of blessing that flows from those who believe.
options.note: True to the image of streams flowing from within, but it does not name the gift as the Spirit, which is the point.

quiz.q: What was the response to Jesus among the crowd and leaders?

options.text: There was division, some believing He was the Christ or the Prophet, others rejecting Him.
options.note: Verses 40 to 43 describe the division Jesus provoked.

options.text: Everyone united in believing He was the Messiah.
options.note: The crowd was split, not united in belief.

options.text: The leaders believed but the crowd rejected Him.
options.note: It was largely the reverse; many in the crowd believed while the rulers scorned Him.

options.text: The crowd was split while the officers marveled that no one ever spoke like Him.
options.note: True and vivid, but it narrows the picture; the division ran through the whole crowd over whether He was the Christ.

quiz.q: How did Nicodemus respond to the leaders' contempt for the crowd?

options.text: He asked whether their law condemns a man without first hearing him.
options.note: Verse 51 records his cautious appeal to fair legal process.

options.text: He openly declared Jesus to be the Christ.
options.note: He did not go that far here; he appealed for a fair hearing rather than confessing Jesus openly.

options.text: He agreed that the crowd was under a curse.
options.note: The opposite; he challenged the leaders rather than joining their contempt.

options.text: He defended Jesus by calling for due process under the law.
options.note: True, but it restates the point without the specific question he raised about hearing a man first.

questions: Even Jesus' own brothers did not believe in Him at first. If you have loved ones who do not yet believe, how does it encourage you that some of them later did?
Jesus said anyone willing to do God's will would recognize His teaching as true. How might your willingness, or unwillingness, to obey be shaping what you are able to believe?
Jesus offered living water to anyone who is thirsty. What thirst in you is He inviting you to bring to Him rather than to lesser wells?
The Spirit makes believers into channels of living water to others. Who around you is thirsty, and how might God want His life to flow through you to them?
The feast forced a division: some came to Jesus, others turned away. What is keeping you from coming to Him fully, and what would it take to come and drink?

translationNotes.note: The Feast of Tabernacles; the BSB footnote explains this is Sukkot, the autumn pilgrim feast recalling the wilderness booths, also called the Feast of Booths or Shelters. Its water and light ceremonies form the backdrop to Jesus' words.
I am not going up to this feast; the BSB footnote notes some manuscripts read I am not yet going up. Jesus declines to go up publicly on His brothers' terms, then goes quietly at the Father's time.
The Spirit had not yet been given; the BSB footnote notes the Greek is literally the Spirit was not yet, meaning the Spirit was not yet poured out, since Jesus had not yet been glorified.
The expectation that the Christ comes from Bethlehem and David's line reflects Micah 5:2, cited in the BSB footnote; the crowd did not know Jesus was in fact born there.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for John 7:1-52. Verse text assembled by concatenating content segments in order; footnote markers and line breaks skipped, embedded speaker turns joined with single spaces. Em dashes in verses 10 (in secret) and 49 (under a curse) were converted to commas. Verse 53 begins the next movement (the woman caught in adultery) and is treated there. No em or en dashes remain. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
